title: Reliability for Ratepayers Act
plain_summary: This law changes how the Bonneville Power Administration can pay and hire its workers. The Bonneville Power Administration is a government agency that sells electricity from dams in the Northwest. The law lets them create a new pay plan that matches what similar electric companies pay their workers. This should help them keep good employees and hire new ones. They have to make this new pay plan within one year and update it every year.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Natural Resources.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Reliability for Ratepayers Act.

sections.title: Section 2  Compensation Flexibility to Address Retention and Hiring Issues at the Bonneville Power Administration
sections.text: This section changes an old law from nineteen thirty seven called the Bonneville Project Act. The Bonneville Power Administration is a government agency that provides electricity in the Pacific Northwest. The administrator is the person who runs this agency. The changes give the administrator new power to set pay for workers. The administrator can now create a pay plan that sets salaries, bonuses, benefits, and other payments for all employees. This includes top managers in the Senior Executive Service, which is a group of high level government workers. The administrator must work with the Director of the Office of Personnel Management to make this pay plan. The Secretary of Energy must also approve the plan, and cannot refuse approval without good reason. The administrator has one year after this law passes to create the first pay plan. Then the administrator has one year after that to start using the new pay plan. The pay plan must be based on a yearly study of what similar jobs pay in public electric companies. The plan must fit within the agency's approved budget and help provide cheap electricity to customers. When setting pay, the administrator must consider education, experience, job responsibilities, where people live, and what is needed to keep good workers and hire new ones. The total pay for the administrator and all employees must be similar to what consumer owned electric companies pay in the Western Interconnection, which is the electric grid system in the western United States. Every year, the administrator must review and update the pay plan if needed. The Secretary of Energy must also review and update the administrator's own pay when appropriate. The administrator must share information about the yearly pay plan review in public business reports. This includes showing the salaries of any employees who earn more than what Level Four executives in the federal government make, which is about one hundred seventy four thousand dollars per year. Each year, the administrator must publish the current pay plan for the public to see. The administrator can hire officers, employees, construction workers, and people to operate and maintain electrical equipment as needed. The administrator can set their pay according to the new pay plan. The administrator does not have to follow certain federal hiring rules found in chapters thirty four, forty three, fifty one, fifty three, fifty seven, and fifty nine of title five of the United States Code. However, employees must still follow merit system principles, which are rules that say government jobs should go to the most qualified people. These principles apply as much as they would to any government owned company. The administrator can hire doctors without following normal civil service rules to do physical exams on current or future workers. The administrator can also hire experts without following civil service rules when needed to do the agency's work.
